questions.stem: A bacterium that is spherical in shape is described by which term?
questions.explanation: A coccus is a spherical or roughly round bacterium, and the plural is cocci. A bacillus is rod-shaped and a spirillum is rigidly spiral. Recognising these basic shapes is the first step in describing bacterial morphology.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A rod-shaped bacterium is described by which term?
questions.explanation: A bacillus is a rod-shaped or cylindrical bacterium, with the plural bacilli. A coccus is spherical, while spirochaetes are long flexible spirals. The word bacillus refers to the shape, although it is also used as a genus name.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A bacterium that is rigid and spiral-shaped is described by which term?
questions.explanation: A spirillum is a rigid, spiral or corkscrew-shaped bacterium, with the plural spirilla. This contrasts with the spherical cocci and the rod-shaped bacilli. Flexible spirals are instead called spirochaetes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The arrangement of cocci in pairs is described by which prefix?
questions.explanation: The prefix diplo means two, so diplococci are cocci arranged in pairs. Strepto refers to chains and staphylo to grape-like clusters. These prefixes describe how cells stay attached after division.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Cocci arranged in chains are described by which prefix?
questions.explanation: The prefix strepto means twisted or chain-like, so streptococci are cocci arranged in chains. This happens when cells divide along a single plane and remain attached end to end. Staphylo describes clusters and diplo describes pairs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Cocci arranged in irregular grape-like clusters are described by which prefix?
questions.explanation: The prefix staphylo refers to a cluster resembling a bunch of grapes, so staphylococci form irregular clusters. These arise when cells divide in several planes and remain attached. Strepto and diplo describe chains and pairs respectively.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The Gram stain divides bacteria into two broad groups based on what property?
options: The structure of their cell wall.
questions.explanation: The Gram stain separates bacteria by differences in the structure of their cell wall, in particular the amount of peptidoglycan present. Gram-positive cells retain the crystal violet dye and appear purple, while Gram-negative cells lose it and take up the counterstain. The test reflects wall structure, not shape, temperature, or nutrition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which feature distinguishes the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria?
options: A thin peptidoglycan layer with an outer membrane.
A wall made entirely of cellulose.
questions.explanation: Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer in their cell wall, which retains the crystal violet stain and gives them a purple appearance. Gram-negative bacteria instead have a thin peptidoglycan layer plus an outer membrane. Bacterial walls are based on peptidoglycan, not cellulose.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A distinctive structural feature of Gram-negative bacteria is the presence of which component?
options: An outer membrane outside a thin peptidoglycan layer.
A thick peptidoglycan layer and no outer membrane.
questions.explanation: Gram-negative bacteria have a thin layer of peptidoglycan surrounded by an additional outer membrane that contains lipopolysaccharide. This outer membrane prevents retention of the crystal violet stain, so the cells appear pink after counterstaining. The thick peptidoglycan and no outer membrane description fits Gram-positive cells instead.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: After the complete Gram-staining procedure, Gram-positive cells appear which colour?
questions.explanation: Gram-positive cells retain the primary crystal violet dye because of their thick peptidoglycan wall and so appear purple or violet at the end of the procedure. Gram-negative cells lose the crystal violet during decolourisation and take up the safranin counterstain, appearing pink or red. Colour is therefore the visible signal of wall structure.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: After the complete Gram-staining procedure, Gram-negative cells appear which colour?
questions.explanation: Gram-negative cells do not retain the crystal violet after decolourisation because their peptidoglycan layer is thin and they have an outer membrane. They instead take up the safranin counterstain and appear pink or red. Purple is reserved for Gram-positive cells.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The macromolecule that forms the rigid framework of the bacterial cell wall is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Peptidoglycan, also called murein, is a mesh of sugars and amino acids that forms the rigid framework of nearly all bacterial cell walls. It provides shape and protects against osmotic lysis. Cellulose is found in plant walls, chitin in fungal walls, and starch is a storage molecule.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A bacterium classified as an obligate aerobe requires which condition to grow?
options: It is killed by even trace oxygen.
It grows equally well with or without oxygen.
questions.explanation: An obligate aerobe requires oxygen for growth because it relies on aerobic respiration to generate energy. Without oxygen it cannot grow. This contrasts with anaerobes, which grow in the absence of oxygen, and facultative organisms, which can do either.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: An obligate anaerobe is a bacterium that does what in the presence of oxygen?
options: Is harmed or killed by oxygen and grows only without it.
Ignores oxygen completely with no effect.
questions.explanation: An obligate anaerobe grows only in the absence of oxygen and is harmed or killed by it, because oxygen and its by-products are toxic to these organisms. They obtain energy by fermentation or anaerobic respiration. Obligate aerobes, by contrast, require oxygen.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A facultative anaerobe is best described by which statement?
options: It can grow only in the complete absence of oxygen.
It is killed by trace amounts of oxygen.
It can grow with or without oxygen, using oxygen when available.
It can grow only in the presence of oxygen.
questions.explanation: A facultative anaerobe can grow either with or without oxygen. It typically uses aerobic respiration when oxygen is present, because that yields more energy, but switches to fermentation or anaerobic respiration when oxygen is absent. This flexibility distinguishes it from strict aerobes and strict anaerobes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: When bacteria are classified by carbon source, an autotroph is defined as an organism that obtains carbon from which source?
options: Pre-formed organic compounds from other organisms.
Only from sunlight, with no carbon needed.
questions.explanation: An autotroph obtains its carbon from inorganic carbon dioxide, building its own organic molecules. This is sometimes called self-feeding. A heterotroph instead takes carbon from pre-formed organic compounds made by other organisms.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A heterotrophic bacterium obtains its carbon from which source?
options: Pre-formed organic compounds from other organisms.
questions.explanation: A heterotroph obtains carbon from pre-formed organic compounds produced by other organisms, since it cannot fix carbon dioxide into organic matter on its own. Most bacteria that cause disease and that decompose organic matter are heterotrophs. Autotrophs, by contrast, build organic molecules from carbon dioxide.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: When bacteria are classified by their preferred growth temperature, a psychrophile is an organism that grows best in which range?
questions.explanation: A psychrophile is a cold-loving organism that grows best at low temperatures, such as those of refrigerators, polar regions, and deep oceans. Mesophiles prefer moderate temperatures, and thermophiles prefer high temperatures. The prefix psychro means cold.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A mesophile grows best at which kind of temperature?
options: Moderate temperatures, including those near human body temperature.
Above the boiling point of water.
questions.explanation: A mesophile grows best at moderate temperatures, typically in the range that includes human body temperature. Most bacteria that live in and on the human body, including many pathogens, are mesophiles. Psychrophiles prefer cold and thermophiles prefer heat.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A thermophile is a bacterium that grows best under which conditions?
options: High temperatures, such as those found in hot springs.
questions.explanation: A thermophile is a heat-loving organism that grows best at high temperatures, such as those of hot springs and compost heaps. The prefix thermo means heat. Psychrophiles prefer cold and mesophiles prefer moderate temperatures.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A bacterium described as a coccus arranged in a chain would most accurately be called which of the following?
questions.explanation: A coccus that occurs in chains is a streptococcus, combining the spherical shape with the chain arrangement signalled by the prefix strepto. A staphylococcus is a cluster of cocci, while a diplobacillus is a pair of rods. A spirillum is a rigid spiral and is not a coccus at all.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which combination correctly classifies a single bacterium along three different schemes?
options: Spherical shape, needs oxygen, prefers heat: an anaerobic coccus.
Spherical shape, requires oxygen, prefers moderate temperature: a mesophilic aerobic coccus.
Rod shape, killed by oxygen, prefers cold: a thermophilic aerobic bacillus.
Spiral shape, no oxygen needed, prefers heat: a psychrophilic spirillum.
questions.explanation: Bacteria are classified independently by shape, oxygen requirement, carbon source, and temperature preference, and the labels must match each scheme. A spherical cell that requires oxygen and prefers moderate temperature is correctly a mesophilic aerobic coccus. The other options mismatch a temperature or oxygen label with the wrong term, for example calling a cold-preferring organism thermophilic or an oxygen-needing organism anaerobic.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following pairs a classification scheme with the correct criterion?
options: Psychrophile versus thermophile is based on oxygen requirement.
Aerobe versus anaerobe is based on shape.
Autotroph versus heterotroph is based on carbon source.
Coccus versus bacillus is based on temperature.
questions.explanation: Autotroph versus heterotroph is indeed a classification by carbon source, autotrophs using carbon dioxide and heterotrophs using organic compounds. Aerobe versus anaerobe is based on oxygen requirement, coccus versus bacillus on shape, and psychrophile versus thermophile on temperature. Matching each scheme to its proper criterion is essential.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which statement about the Gram stain is correct?
options: It measures the size of a bacterium.
It only works on eukaryotic cells.
It is a differential stain that separates bacteria into Gram-positive and Gram-negative groups.
questions.explanation: The Gram stain is a differential staining technique that separates bacteria into Gram-positive and Gram-negative groups according to differences in their cell walls. It is one of the most widely used first steps in identifying bacteria. It does not measure size and is applied to bacteria rather than to viruses or eukaryotic cells.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Complete the description of bacterial shapes and arrangements by selecting the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: A spherical bacterium is called a {{1}}, while a rod-shaped one is called a {{2}}. When spherical cells remain attached in grape-like clusters, the arrangement is described by the prefix {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A spherical bacterium is a coccus and a rod is a bacillus. The prefix staphylo describes a grape-like cluster, in contrast to strepto for chains and diplo for pairs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Complete the summary of the Gram stain by choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: Gram-positive bacteria have a {{1}} layer of peptidoglycan and appear {{2}} at the end of the staining procedure. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer plus an additional outer {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Gram-positive cells have a thick peptidoglycan layer and retain the crystal violet, appearing purple. Gram-negative cells have a thin peptidoglycan layer and an extra outer membrane, and appear pink after counterstaining.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Classify each bacterium by its oxygen requirement, choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: A bacterium that requires oxygen to grow is an {{1}}. A bacterium that grows only in the absence of oxygen and is harmed by it is an obligate {{2}}. A bacterium that can grow either with or without oxygen is a {{3}} anaerobe.
questions.explanation: An organism that requires oxygen is an aerobe, while one that grows only without oxygen and is harmed by it is an obligate anaerobe. A facultative anaerobe can grow either way, using oxygen when it is available.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Classify each bacterium by carbon source or temperature preference, choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: A bacterium that builds its own organic molecules from carbon dioxide is an {{1}}. A bacterium that grows best at high temperatures such as those of hot springs is a {{2}}. A bacterium that grows best at moderate temperatures near human body heat is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: An autotroph fixes carbon dioxide into organic molecules, unlike a heterotroph. A thermophile prefers high temperatures, while a mesophile prefers moderate temperatures near body heat.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)
